articles.title: Influence of age-related bone density changes on primary stability in stemless shoulder arthroplasty: a multi-implant finite element study.
articles.authors: Libano Monteiro H, Antunes M, Sarmento M, Quental C, Folgado J
articles.study_type: Finite element analysis study using computational modelling
articles.population: Virtual shoulder models based on bone density data from 40 subjects aged 20-40 and 60-80 years
articles.sample_size: 20 subjects from two age groups (40 total); five implant designs tested
articles.outcomes: Micromotion at bone-implant interface under eight different loading conditions; assessment against osseointegration thresholds of 20, 50, and 150 micrometres
articles.take_home: Older patients with lower bone density showed more movement at the implant-bone junction, but some stemless shoulder implant designs performed well regardless of age, suggesting age alone should not prevent patients from having this type of surgery. Certain designs and positioning methods reduced unwanted movement.

articles.title: Evaluation of new normal after shoulder arthroplasty: comparison of anatomic vs. reverse total shoulder arthroplasty.
articles.authors: Beleckas CM, Schodlbauer DF, Mousad AD, Levy JC
articles.population: Patients who had primary shoulder replacement surgery (anatomic or reverse type) between 2006 and 2021
articles.sample_size: 1594 patients (849 anatomic, 745 reverse) who perceived their shoulder as close to normal at follow-up
articles.outcomes: Patient satisfaction scores, range of motion, ability to lift and carry objects, ability to reach high shelves, ability to return to work and sport
articles.take_home: Amongst patients who felt their shoulder was nearly normal after surgery, those who had anatomic shoulder replacement performed better than those who had reverse shoulder replacement, with better movement and greater ability to do demanding activities like lifting and sports. Anatomic replacement gave a 40% chance of feeling the shoulder was normal compared to 26% for reverse replacement.

articles.title: Long-term outcomes of reverse shoulder arthroplasty versus nonoperative treatment for 3- or 4-part proximal humerus fractures in elderly patients: results from a prior randomized clinical trial.
articles.authors: Lopiz Y, Alcobia-Diaz B, Coderch J, Rodrigo-Muro S, Garcia-Fernandez C, Echevarria-Marin M, et al.
articles.study_type: Randomised controlled trial with long-term follow-up
articles.population: Patients aged 80 years or older with 3- or 4-part displaced proximal humerus fractures
articles.outcomes: Constant score, Disabilities of the Arm Shoulder and Hand questionnaire, Short Form 12 physical and mental health scores, pain scores, radiographic findings, need for revision surgery
articles.take_home: Reverse shoulder arthroplasty provided better shoulder function at long-term follow-up (average 7.5 years) compared to nonoperative treatment in very elderly patients with complex fractures. Nonoperative treatment led to fractures healing in poor position, though patients did not require later surgery.

articles.title: Stemless anatomic and reverse shoulder arthroplasty in patients under 55 years of age with primary glenohumeral osteoarthritis: an analysis of the Australian Orthopedic Association National Joint Replacement Registry at 5 years.
articles.authors: Jomaa M, Ingoe H, Hollman F, Pareyon R, Whitehouse SL, Du P, et al.
articles.population: Patients under 55 years of age undergoing shoulder arthroplasty for primary glenohumeral osteoarthritis
articles.outcomes: Cumulative per cent revision rates at 6 years for different arthroplasty types; reasons for revision; hazard ratios comparing revision rates between procedures
articles.take_home: Reverse shoulder arthroplasty and stemless anatomic arthroplasty had the lowest revision rates (6.5% and 7.0% respectively at 6 years) in young patients with osteoarthritis, making them better options than stemmed anatomic arthroplasty which had higher revision rates. Loosening and instability were the main reasons for revision depending on the type of implant used.

articles.title: Long-term (minimum 10 years) survival and outcomes of pyrocarbon interposition shoulder arthroplasty.
articles.authors: Barret H, Garret J, Favard L, Bonnevialle N, Collin P, Gauci MO, et al.
articles.study_type: Retrospective review of prospectively collected data
articles.population: Young and active patients with shoulder osteoarthritis (primary, post-traumatic, or post-instability) who underwent pyrocarbon interposition shoulder arthroplasty
articles.sample_size: 71 patients for survival analysis; 62 patients (62 shoulders) for clinical and radiologic outcomes
articles.outcomes: Prosthesis survival rate, revision surgery rates, Constant score, Subjective Shoulder Value, glenoid wear patterns, and complications including glenoid erosion and rotator cuff tears
articles.take_home: Pyrocarbon shoulder implants were successful for about 87% of patients over 10 years, with good pain relief and function in those not requiring revision surgery. However, patients with certain glenoid bone shapes (biconcave type) had a much higher failure rate (44%) and were more likely to need revision surgery.

articles.title: Acromial stress fractures and reactions after reverse total shoulder arthroplasty: a case-control study.
articles.authors: Wiemer F, Coghlan JA, Bell SN
articles.population: Patients who underwent reverse total shoulder arthroplasty
articles.sample_size: 220 rTSAs (24 cases with acromial stress fractures or reactions, 48 matched controls)
articles.outcomes: Incidence of acromial stress fractures and reactions, risk factors, functional outcomes measured by American Shoulder and Elbow Surgeons Shoulder score, Shoulder Pain and Disability Index, and Disabilities of the Arm Shoulder and Hand scores
articles.take_home: Acromial stress fractures or reactions occurred in 11% of patients after reverse shoulder replacement, typically around 5-6 months after surgery. Patients taking corticosteroids or who had previous shoulder surgery were at higher risk, and those who developed this complication had worse shoulder function and movement compared to those who did not.

articles.title: B2 and B3 glenoid osteoarthirtis: outcomes of corrective and concentric (C2) reaming of the glenoid combined with pyrocarbon hemiarthroplasty.
articles.authors: Ranieri R, Cointat C, Lacouture-Suarez JD, Boileau P
articles.population: Patients with B2 and B3 glenoid osteoarthritis undergoing shoulder surgery
articles.sample_size: 41 shoulders in 35 patients, mean age 57.9 years
articles.outcomes: Glenoid version, posterior humeral subluxation, glenoid erosion progression, functional scores (Constant Score and Subjective Shoulder Value), return to activities, prosthesis survival, and revision rates
articles.take_home: At 4.5 years follow-up, this surgery combining special glenoid reaming with a pyrocarbon implant successfully reduced the abnormal positioning of the upper arm bone and prevented painful glenoid erosion, with 95% of implants surviving and patients showing excellent functional improvement and return to work and sports.

articles.title: Inconsistencies in Terminology Used to Describe Reverse Shoulder Arthroplasty: A Systematic Review.
articles.authors: Werthel JD, Descamps J, Lugo E, Routman H
articles.study_type: Systematic review of literature reporting patterns
articles.population: Peer-reviewed studies on reverse shoulder arthroplasty implant design published after 2015
articles.outcomes: Consistency and clarity of terminology used to describe implant design features including centre of rotation position, glenoid and humeral offset parameters, and design classification
articles.take_home: Research papers describing reverse shoulder arthroplasty implants use inconsistent and poorly defined terminology, with less than one-fifth clearly explaining key design features. This makes it difficult for doctors and researchers to compare different implants and their results across studies.

articles.title: The safety of percutaneous screw placement in reverse shoulder arthroplasty for fracture: A cadaveric study.
articles.authors: Tantone RP, Dobyns A, Regal S
articles.outcomes: Distance from drill bits and trocars to axillary and radial nerves during percutaneous screw placement in reverse shoulder arthroplasty
articles.take_home: During reverse shoulder surgery for fractures, the drill used for screw placement comes very close to important nerves, with the drill touching or entering the radial nerve in some cases. Surgeons need to be careful when placing these screws to avoid damaging these nerves.

articles.title: Long-term corticosteroid use elevates 90-day medical complications but not 5-year surgical complications after shoulder arthroplasty.
articles.authors: Holmstrom JA, Hiredesai AN, Holle AM, Braithwaite CL, Hassebrock JD
articles.population: Patients undergoing primary total shoulder arthroplasty with minimum 5-year follow-up
articles.sample_size: 556 long-term corticosteroid users matched 1:10 with 4942 controls
articles.outcomes: 90-day medical complications (pneumonia and venous thromboembolism) and 5-year surgical complications
articles.take_home: Patients taking long-term corticosteroids had nearly three times higher risk of pneumonia and more than twice the risk of blood clots within 90 days after shoulder replacement surgery. However, their chances of surgical complications at 5 years were similar to patients not taking corticosteroids.

articles.title: Cross-cultural adaptation and validation of the Arabic version of the Pennsylvania Shoulder Score.
articles.authors: Boutros M, Awad G, Hassan C, Hammad S, Leba A, Kais S, et al.
articles.population: Adults aged 18 years and over with shoulder pain or dysfunction
articles.outcomes: Internal consistency, readability, convergent validity with Arabic Simple Shoulder Test, test-retest reliability, minimal detectable change
articles.take_home: The Arabic version of the Pennsylvania Shoulder Score is a reliable and valid tool for measuring shoulder pain, function and satisfaction in Arabic-speaking patients. The questionnaire is easy to read and produces consistent results when patients complete it again after a few days.

articles.title: Predictors of Loss to Long-Term Follow-up After Shoulder Surgery.
articles.authors: Soule S, Smith A, Staten T, Kirkham M, Zhang C, Presson A, et al.
articles.outcomes: Completion of 2-year follow-up after shoulder surgery; predictors of loss to follow-up
articles.take_home: Younger patients were significantly less likely to complete 2-year follow-up after shoulder surgery compared with older patients, particularly those undergoing instability procedures or covered by Medicaid or Workers' Compensation. Targeted follow-up strategies may be needed for these higher-risk groups.

articles.title: Effect of an Atelocollagen-Loaded Hyaluronic Acid-Based Porous Scaffold on Healing in a Rabbit Model of Chronic Rotator Cuff Tear.
articles.authors: Rhee SM, Im P, Im J, Kim MS, Ko JH, Oh JH, et al.
articles.study_type: Controlled laboratory study with in vivo animal model
articles.population: Rabbits with chronic rotator cuff tears
articles.outcomes: Collagen fibre density, tendon stiffness, load-to-failure, gene expression, and biocompatibility at 12 weeks post-repair
articles.take_home: A new scaffold made from hyaluronic acid and collagen significantly improved collagen organisation and tendon stiffness in a rabbit rotator cuff tear model. This scaffold may help improve healing after rotator cuff surgery in humans, but further research is needed.

articles.title: Thirty-Six Months of Experience and 30 degrees of Hip Internal Rotation as Critical Thresholds for Capitellar Osteochondritis Dissecans in Youth Baseball Players.
articles.authors: Kamiya T, Nagasawa M, Oota T, Tajima T, Kamei N
articles.outcomes: Capitellar osteochondritis dissecans (OCD) diagnosed by ultrasonography and radiography
articles.take_home: Young baseball players with 36 months or more of competitive play and less than 30 degrees of hip internal rotation on their non-dominant side are at higher risk of developing OCD. These measurements could help doctors identify which players need closer monitoring for this serious elbow condition.

articles.title: Adjunctive Hybrid Telerehabilitation Is Associated With Comparable Clinical Outcomes to In-Person Physical Therapy After Shoulder Arthroplasty and Arthroscopic Rotator Cuff Repair: A Cohort Study.
articles.authors: Gangavaram A, Mahatme RJ, Moore SA, Keller J, Kabo I, Reddy E, et al.
articles.population: Adults undergoing shoulder arthroplasty or arthroscopic rotator cuff repair
articles.sample_size: 207 patients (85 in arthroplasty cohort, 122 in rotator cuff repair cohort)
articles.outcomes: American Shoulder and Elbow Surgeons score, pain score, range of motion, physiotherapy utilisation, complications and reoperations
articles.take_home: Adding remote telerehabilitation to standard physiotherapy after shoulder surgery produced similar improvements in pain, function and movement compared to physiotherapy alone, but patients needed fewer weeks of treatment and fewer clinic visits. Complication rates were comparable between both approaches.

articles.title: Preclinical Safety and Efficacy of an Allogeneic Adipose-Derived Mesenchymal Stem Cell Medicinal Product for Rotator Cuff Repair: A Two-Phase Experimental Study.
articles.authors: Lopiz Y, Ponz-Lueza V, Arvinius C, Rodriguez-Bobada C, Rojo-Perez F, Tornero-Esteban P, et al.
articles.study_type: Preclinical experimental study in two phases
articles.population: Phase I: 164 immunodeficient mice; Phase II: 48 rats with chronic rotator cuff tears
articles.outcomes: Phase I: toxicity, biodistribution, tumorigenicity. Phase II: histological healing and biomechanical strength of repaired tendons
articles.take_home: The stem cell treatment was safe at all doses tested in mice, but when injected into repaired rotator cuff tears in rats, it did not improve healing compared to placebo. The poor results were likely due to cell loss, immune rejection, and movement of cells away from the repair site.
